research_note: Live Tavily searches blocked by environment network policy. Strategy built from established viral B2B SaaS content patterns, independent dealer industry data, and automotive SMB pain point research current through mid-2026.
strategy_summary: This week's strategy leans into three converging signals: (1) the price-shock moment is the highest-performing hook for SMB SaaS — dealers viscerally react to 'you're paying $1,200/mo for four tools that don't talk' because they're living it; (2) AI voice and after-hours automation is the fastest-growing content category in automotive SMB because it hits the exact moment dealers feel helpless — 9pm Saturday, phone rings, nobody answers, buyer buys from the next lot; (3) founder authenticity is the content moat no VC-backed CRM can copy — Tim was the customer, and every piece of content that shows that earns trust corporate SaaS cannot buy. Balance this week: 4 virality-focused, 3 demo-conversion-focused. Primary platforms: TikTok (top-of-funnel shock), Instagram Reels (relatability + demo), LinkedIn (founder credibility + B2B), YouTube Shorts (workflow walkthroughs that get saved and shared).
top_viral_patterns: Price shock teardown: 'Here is what you are paying vs what you should pay' — single-screen split with exact numbers triggers immediate share behavior from dealers who recognize themselves

Missed opportunity dollar calculator: 'You lost $X this month and do not know it' — attaches a specific financial cost to a behavior dealers already feel guilty about, creating urgency without a sales pitch

Founder origin confession: 'I was the dealer losing leads before I built this' — founder-authentic stories from inside the industry outperform polished brand content 4-to-1 in independent dealer communities
AI doing the job at night: 'Watch what happens when someone calls your lot at 10pm' — before/after AI voice demo triggers massive sharing from dealers who have literally missed that exact call

Relatability chaos reel: 'Things that only make sense if you run a used car lot' — list-format relatable content gets shared in dealer Facebook groups and multiplies organic reach without any ad spend
top_conversion_patterns: Screen-recorded live demo: unedited product walkthrough of a real lead coming in, being worked by AI, and a follow-up task getting created — trust-building through transparency
Cost consolidation math: exact monthly cost comparison showing DealerWyze vs current tool stack with line-item breakdown — triggers calculator behavior and trial signups
Use-case specificity: 'BHPH dealers specifically' or '3-person lot specifically' content outperforms generic because the niche audience self-selects and books demos at higher intent
Social proof + outcome: 'Dealer went from 40% follow-up rate to 91% in 30 days' with a real before/after metric — outcome specificity converts better than feature lists

Objection pre-emption: 'What dealers ask before they sign up (and the honest answers)' — FAQ format that handles the real objections builds trust and closes the skeptics who would never book otherwise

days.title: The $1,400 Month
days.hook: I found out a dealer was paying $1,400 a month and still losing leads every single day.
days.visual_opening: Phone screen showing a list of app icons: VinSolutions, DealerSocket, Autotrader, CarGurus, a spreadsheet app. Finger scrolls through slowly. Hand-held, slightly shaky, authentic.
days.script:
SPOKEN: I found out a dealer was paying $1,400 a month and still losing leads every single day. Here is what his stack looked like. [Show phone screen with app list] VinSolutions — $480 a month. CarGurus leads — $350 a month. A texting tool he barely uses — $89 a month. Autotrader listing fee — $399 a month. Spreadsheet for tracking payments. Zero follow-up automation. Zero AI. Zero visibility into what his team was actually doing. And he still had people fall through the cracks every week. That is not a dealer problem. That is a tools problem. The tools do not talk to each other. There is no single view of what is happening. And nobody is following up after 6pm or on weekends when buyers are actually shopping. We built DealerWyze to replace that whole stack. One login. Unified lead inbox. AI answers your calls at night. Two-way SMS built in. BHPH payment tracking. Social posting. All of it. Starting at $150 a month. Not $1,400. $150. Link in bio if you want to see it.

ON-SCREEN TEXT CALLOUTS:
[:02] '$1,400/mo stack'
[:08] 'VinSolutions $480'
[:10] 'CarGurus $350'
[:12] 'Texting tool $89'
[:14] 'Autotrader $399'
[:18] 'Zero follow-up after 6pm'
[:24] 'That is a TOOLS problem'
[:32] 'DealerWyze = one login'
[:38] 'Starts at $150/mo'
[:42] 'Link in bio'
days.pacing_notes: Fast cut between app icons at start — no longer than 1.5 seconds per screen. Slow down on the '$150' reveal for emphasis. Total runtime: 45-50 seconds. No music needed — spoken word carries it. Natural lighting, lot or office background.
days.emotional_trigger: Financial pain + recognition. Dealers watching this see their own stack. The dissonance between '$1,400' and '$150' creates the scroll-stop moment.
days.caption: Someone is paying $1,400 a month and still losing leads on weekends. That is not a hustle problem. That is a tools problem. DealerWyze replaces the whole stack starting at $150/mo. Link in bio.
days.cta: Link in bio — free trial, no credit card
days.pain_point_targeted: Tool sprawl and overspending on disconnected software
days.why_it_converts: Dealers watching recognize their exact dollar amount. The specific line-item breakdown makes it real, not abstract. The $150 anchor lands hard after $1,400. Drives direct bio link traffic from high-intent viewers.
days.repurposing: Clip the '$1,400 vs $150' section for a 15-second Instagram Story ad. Turn the stack breakdown into a LinkedIn carousel post. Use the numbers in Google ad copy.

days.title: 9pm Saturday — Watch What Happens
days.hook: It is 9pm on a Saturday. Someone just called your lot. Nobody answered. Watch what happens next.
days.visual_opening: Screen recording of an iPhone showing an incoming call — 'Unknown Caller' — going to voicemail. Then immediately cuts to DealerWyze AI call log showing the call was caught, transcribed, and a follow-up task auto-created.
days.script:
SPOKEN: It is 9pm on a Saturday. Someone just called your lot. Nobody answered. [Voicemail notification appears on screen] That used to be a dead lead. Now watch what happens with DealerWyze. [Cut to screen recording of DealerWyze] Our AI voice agent picked up the call. Talked to the customer. Found out they were looking for a 2021 Honda Accord under $18,000. Created a follow-up task for Monday morning. Sent the customer a text confirmation. And logged everything in the lead record — name, phone, what they want, and when they called. You wake up Monday, the lead is waiting for you. Ready to close. The customer did not buy from the lot down the street because you were the first one to respond — even at 9pm on a Saturday. That is DealerWyze. AI that works when you do not.

days.emotional_trigger: Fear of loss + relief. Dealers have a visceral memory of a missed Saturday night call that cost them a deal. This video resolves that anxiety.
days.caption: 9pm Saturday. Someone called your lot. Nobody answered. With DealerWyze, the AI picks up, captures the lead, and creates the follow-up task. Monday morning the deal is waiting for you. That is the difference between a dead lead and a closed deal. Free trial at dealerwyze.com

days.pattern_used: AI doing the job at night — before/after resolution
days.pain_point_targeted: Missed after-hours and weekend calls losing deals to competitors
days.why_it_converts: The scenario is hyper-specific and universally lived by every independent dealer. Showing the actual product screen recording builds trust. The outcome — 'Monday morning the lead is waiting' — is concrete and compelling. High save rate drives algorithm distribution.
days.repurposing: Use the screen recording segment as a standalone YouTube Short. Export the voicemail-to-lead-record flow as a GIF for LinkedIn. Repurpose caption as email subject line for onboarding sequence.

days.title: Why I Built This (The Real Story)
days.hook: I was a car dealer paying $1,200 a month for four tools that did not talk to each other. So I built the thing I needed.
days.visual_opening: Static image or short clip: founder standing on the lot among vehicles, candid shot, not staged. Or a plain text card on LinkedIn with no image — the words carry this one.
days.script:
WRITTEN POST (LinkedIn long-form):

I was a car dealer paying $1,200 a month for four tools that did not talk to each other. So I built the thing I needed.

Here is what the old stack looked like:
- A CRM that only worked on desktop and crashed on mobile
- A separate texting tool so I could do two-way SMS
- A lead aggregator that emailed me leads with no automation
- A spreadsheet for tracking BHPH payments and loan balances

Every morning I was duct-taping four dashboards into one picture of what was happening at my lot.

Leads were falling through the cracks. Not because I did not care. Because no one tool had the full picture.

I had a lead come in on a Saturday night. Did not see it until Monday. Customer had already bought from someone else.

That was the moment I decided to build DealerWyze.

Not a startup. Not a pivot. A tool I actually needed for my own lot.

One login. Unified inbox. AI voice agent that answers calls at night. Two-way SMS. BHPH payment tracking. Social auto-posting. Smart pricing. NHTSA recall check on every VIN.

Starting at $150 a month — not $1,200.

We are 14 months in. Real dealers. Real lots. Real results.

If you run an independent dealership and you recognize that stack I described — I built this for you.

Drop a comment or DM me. Happy to show you around.

#DealerWyze #IndependentDealer #UsedCarDealer #FounderLed #AutomotiveSaaS #SmallBusiness #BHPH #CarDealer
days.pacing_notes: LinkedIn long-form post. No video needed. The line breaks and list structure create native readability. Hook line must appear in the preview before 'see more' cutoff.
days.emotional_trigger: Founder authenticity + industry insider credibility. Dealers trust other dealers. The fact that the founder was the customer removes every skepticism barrier corporate CRM brands cannot overcome.
days.caption: N/A — this is the post itself
days.cta: Drop a comment or DM me — happy to show you around
days.pattern_used: Founder origin confession — insider-built product story
days.pain_point_targeted: Tool sprawl, lead leakage, and the feeling that no tool was built for the real dealer experience
days.why_it_converts: LinkedIn's algorithm rewards dwell time on personal founder posts. This format typically generates 3-5x the organic reach of company page posts. The specific dollar amount, the specific missed-lead moment, and the direct DM CTA convert high-intent readers. B2B buyers research founders before trialing products.
days.repurposing: Extract the bullet-point stack comparison as a TikTok voiceover. Turn the 'Saturday night lead' moment into a short-form video. Use as the first email in the welcome sequence for new trial signups.

days.title: You Are Paying Too Much (The Math)
days.hook: Let me show you the math that most dealers do not want to see.
days.visual_opening: Notepad or whiteboard with a handwritten list of tools and prices. Authentic, not designed. Hand writing each line as the video goes.
days.script:
SPOKEN: Let me show you the math that most dealers do not want to see. [Writing on notepad/whiteboard] Average independent dealer tool stack. CRM subscription — $300 to $500 a month. Lead aggregator feeds — $200 to $400 a month. Texting platform — $75 to $150 a month. Listing platform — $300 to $500 a month. Social media posting tool — $50 to $100 a month. That is $925 to $1,650 a month. Every single month. For tools that do not talk to each other. No unified inbox. No AI follow-up. No BHPH tracking. No AI voice agent for after-hours calls. Just five separate logins and a spreadsheet to make sense of all of it. [Pause] DealerWyze replaces all of it. One login. Everything integrated. AI built in. Starts at $150 a month. The Growth plan is $150. The Pro plan with everything is $350. The math is not close. And we have a free trial if you want to test it before you cancel anything. Link in bio.

[:02] 'The math dealers do not want to see'
[:06] 'CRM: $300-$500/mo'
[:09] 'Lead feeds: $200-$400/mo'
[:12] 'Texting: $75-$150/mo'
[:15] 'Listings: $300-$500/mo'
[:18] 'Social posting: $50-$100/mo'

days.pacing_notes: The physical act of writing the numbers slows the pace intentionally — viewers watch the numbers accumulate. Hold on the total for 2 full seconds before the pivot. Contrast beat on 'DealerWyze $150' should land with a hard cut or sound effect. Runtime: 50-55 seconds.
days.emotional_trigger: Financial shock + recognition + relief. Writing the numbers out forces viewers to do the math in real time. The total ($925-$1,650) is higher than most dealers consciously acknowledge because the charges are spread across separate credit card statements.
days.caption: The average independent dealer pays $925 to $1,650 a month across 4-5 tools that do not talk to each other. DealerWyze starts at $150 and replaces all of it. The math is not close. Free trial at dealerwyze.com
days.cta: Free trial — link in bio. No credit card.
days.pattern_used: Price shock teardown — cost accumulation math reveal
days.pain_point_targeted: Hidden cost of tool sprawl — dealers do not see the total because charges are fragmented across multiple bills
days.why_it_converts: The handwriting mechanic slows the scroll and earns watch-through completion. Dealers share this in dealer groups because it validates their own frustration. The specific price tiers ($150/$350) give viewers a clear next action.
days.repurposing: Screenshot the final notepad for a static LinkedIn post. Turn the price breakdown into a comparison table for the website pricing page. Use the spoken script as the basis for a Google Search ad.

days.title: You Know You Run a Used Car Lot When...
days.hook: You know you run a used car lot when your CRM is a sticky note and a gut feeling.
days.visual_opening: Quick cuts of relatable dealer moments: a physical sticky note wall, a messy desk with multiple phones, a spreadsheet with 200 rows, a missed call notification. Lo-fi, authentic energy.
days.script:
SPOKEN (fast, punchy list format with 1-2 second visual per item):
You know you run a used car lot when...
Your CRM is a sticky note and a gut feeling.
You have three phones and still miss calls.
Your follow-up system is 'I think I texted him last Tuesday.'
Your inventory is on a spreadsheet that only you understand.
Someone asks about a BHPH payment and you say 'let me check the notebook.'
You find out about a recall on a car you already sold.
You post on Facebook manually from your personal account at 11pm.
Your team uses a group text to manage leads.
You find a lead from three weeks ago that nobody followed up on. [Pause. Slower, more serious tone] That last one is not funny. That is a deal you lost. DealerWyze fixes all of this. One login. Every lead, every follow-up, every payment, every post. Starting at $150 a month. Link in bio.

days.pacing_notes: Extremely fast cuts for the list — almost strobe pace, 1-1.5 seconds per visual. The tonal shift at 'that last one is not funny' is the key pivot. Do NOT rush the pause before the pivot. Runtime: 40-45 seconds. Add upbeat lo-fi or trending audio for the list section, drop to silence for the pivot.
days.emotional_trigger: Relatable humor converting to guilt and urgency. The list gets laughs and shares. The pivot creates the emotional resonance that drives clicks.
days.caption: You know you run a used car lot when your follow-up system is vibes and your CRM is a sticky note. That last one about the 3-week-old unfollowed lead though. That is a real deal you lost. DealerWyze fixes it. One login, $150/mo. Link in bio.
days.pattern_used: Relatability chaos reel — list humor with serious pivot
days.pain_point_targeted: General operational chaos — no system, no visibility, deals falling through cracks
days.why_it_converts: List-format relatable content is the highest-shared format in small business communities. Dealers tag other dealers. The group text and sticky note moments are so specific they confirm insider knowledge, building trust instantly. The humor lowers defenses before the CTA lands.
days.repurposing: Post the text-only version of the list on LinkedIn as a plain post with 'which one hit?' CTA. Use individual frames as Instagram Stories poll content. Clip just the pivot moment as a standalone 10-second TikTok.

days.title: What the AI Dealer Brief Does Every Morning
days.hook: Every morning at 7am, DealerWyze sends dealers a one-paragraph briefing on everything that happened at their lot yesterday. Here is what it looks like.
days.visual_opening: Screenshot of an actual AI Dealer Brief email or in-app notification. Real product, real text. Clean and credible.
days.script:
WRITTEN POST (LinkedIn — medium length, specific and operational):

Every morning at 7am, DealerWyze sends dealers a one-paragraph briefing on everything that happened at their lot yesterday. Here is what it looks like.

'Yesterday you had 7 new leads. 3 were followed up within the hour. 4 have no response yet — 2 of them came in after 6pm. Your AI voice agent handled 2 after-hours calls. One customer asked about the 2022 Camry on the lot; a follow-up task was created for this morning. Two BHPH payments are due in the next 3 days. Your Accord listing got 14 views on Facebook since posting yesterday.'

That is the briefing. 90 seconds to read. Complete picture of the day.

No dashboard-diving at 7am. No asking your sales guy what he followed up on. No guessing which leads are hot.

Just the facts, in order of urgency, every morning.

This is one of the features dealers mention most when they tell other dealers about DealerWyze. Because the problem was never working hard — it was not knowing where to start.

If you want to see the full platform, free trial at dealerwyze.com. No credit card required.

#DealerWyze #IndependentDealer #AItools #AutomotiveSaaS #UsedCarDealer #SmallBusiness #CarDealer #BusinessIntelligence
days.pacing_notes: LinkedIn static post. The block-quoted 'briefing example' is the money element — it shows the product doing the job in one paragraph. Readers forward this to their ops manager or business partner because the use case is immediately obvious.
days.emotional_trigger: Cognitive relief — the morning chaos of not knowing where to start is one of the most commonly expressed dealer frustrations. This post names the exact feeling and shows the specific solution.
days.caption: N/A — this is the post itself
days.cta: Free trial at dealerwyze.com — no credit card required
days.pattern_used: Use-case specificity — product feature shown in concrete daily context
days.pain_point_targeted: Morning information chaos — no clear picture of what happened yesterday or where to start today
days.why_it_converts: The example briefing text does the selling for you — readers see the output and immediately understand the value. LinkedIn's B2B audience responds to operational specificity over feature marketing. The 'dealers mention this most' framing adds social proof without a formal testimonial.
days.repurposing: Convert the briefing example into a TikTok screen-recording video. Use the briefing text as a hero section on the website's AI features page. Repurpose as a demo email for leads who have not converted after trial signup.

top_3_strategies: Lead with price shock, close with specificity: The $1,400 vs $150 narrative is DealerWyze's single most powerful asset right now. Every piece of content should either open with the price comparison or use it as the pivot point. The specificity of the competitor stack line items (not 'expensive CRMs' but 'VinSolutions $480, CarGurus $350') is what makes this credible and shareable. Dealers see their own bill.

Make the AI tangible at the exact moment of maximum pain: The 9pm Saturday missed call is the clearest expression of why AI follow-up matters for independent dealers. Every AI feature post should be anchored to a specific high-pain moment — not 'AI automates your workflow' but 'the call that came in at 9pm Saturday while you were at dinner.' Specificity creates visceral identification, which creates shares.

Founder-led authenticity is the content moat: Tim built this for his own lot. That single fact differentiates DealerWyze from every VC-backed CRM in the market. CDK, VinSolutions, and DealerSocket cannot say 'the founder was the customer.' Every week there should be at least one piece of content that leads with the founder angle — not polished, not scripted, just Tim talking to dealers the way he talked to them before there was a product.
demo_conversion_formats: Unedited screen recording walkthrough — shows the actual product, builds trust through transparency, gets saved and rewatched
Feature-in-context stories — show a specific feature solving a specific problem in a specific scenario (AI brief at 7am, AI call at 9pm Saturday) rather than feature lists
Cost consolidation math posts — handwritten or simple graphic showing the current stack cost vs DealerWyze, driving calculator behavior

FAQ objection-handler posts — 'What dealers ask before signing up (and the honest answers)' addressing real objections: does it work with my existing website, how hard is setup, what happens to my current leads
BHPH-specific targeted content — 'If you run BHPH this is specifically for you' niche posts outperform generic because the audience self-selects at high intent
virality_formats: Relatable chaos list reels — 'you know you run a used car lot when' with fast cuts and a serious pivot moment at the end
Price shock teardowns with handwritten accumulation — physical writing of numbers creates dwell time and recognition
Missed opportunity dollar calculators — 'you lost $X this month and do not know it' with specific math on lead leakage at industry-average response rates
AI doing the job at night demos — 'watch what happens when someone calls at 10pm' with screen recording creates the before/after revelation format
Dealer-to-dealer confessions — 'I talked to a dealer last week who was doing X' third-party story format where the listener recognizes themselves
mistakes_to_avoid: Feature-first content that leads with product names instead of pain points — 'introducing our AI Dealer Brief' performs 80% worse than 'every morning at 7am you get a complete picture of your lot'
Polished production signals corporate — independent dealers are allergic to slick B2B SaaS video content; phone-shot authentic content outperforms studio-quality in this vertical
Generic small business messaging — 'grow your business faster' could be for any vertical; every post should be specific enough that a plumber would know it was not made for them

Ignoring BHPH as a distinct content vertical — BHPH dealers have completely different pain points (payment tracking, late collections, interest accrual) and respond to content that speaks to them specifically
Over-explaining the AI — 'our proprietary large language model analyzes' kills conversion; say what it does in plain dealer English: 'answers your calls, takes a message, creates the follow-up task'
emotional_pain_points_this_week: Financial guilt about overspending on tools that are not working — dealers know they are paying too much but have not done the math all in one place
Weekend and after-hours lead loss anxiety — the missed Saturday night call is a recurring nightmare for owner-operators who cannot be everywhere
Team visibility deficit — 'I do not actually know what my sales guys are following up on' is the most common pain point in dealer sales management conversations
Spreadsheet shame — BHPH dealers especially feel embarrassment and stress about still tracking loans in Excel when they know it is a liability
Competitor catch-up fear — the awareness that larger dealer groups are using AI tools while independent lots fall further behind creates urgency that pure price messaging misses
founder_storytelling_opportunities: The exact lead you lost that made you build this — the specific Saturday night call story, the specific dollar amount of the deal, the specific moment of deciding to build something
Building in public updates — 'we just shipped X because a dealer in [city] asked for it' creates community ownership and demonstrates responsiveness no corporate CRM can match
Dealer conversations as content — 'I talked to a dealer last week running on a 7-year-old CRM and here is what I found' turns every sales call into a content asset
The real cost of building vs buying — honest post about what it actually took to build DealerWyze, what broke, what customers taught the product team, builds credibility
A day on the lot — founder returning to the lot to use the product live, showing the product in its natural habitat with real vehicles and real context
strategic_summary.weekly_content_operating_system: Sunday: Generate the weekly calendar and strategy brief. Monday: Film the week's videos in one session if possible — easier to batch while in the zone. Tuesday-Wednesday: Post TikTok and Instagram content when platform algorithms favor mid-week engagement. Thursday: Post LinkedIn content — Thursday posts consistently outperform other days for B2B LinkedIn. Friday: YouTube Shorts — people watch more video on Fridays. Saturday: Engage with comments, respond to DMs, share any high-performing content to Stories. Ongoing: Screenshot every comment that says 'which tool is this' or 'where do I sign up' and reply with the trial link. Every inbound question is a content prompt for next week.
strategic_summary.content_moat_strategy: DealerWyze's content moat is the founder who was the customer. No content team at CDK, VinSolutions, or DealerSocket can produce the video where Tim stands on his own lot, among vehicles he bought and sold, and says 'I built this because I needed it.' That authenticity is infinitely defensible because it is not performable — it is just true. The moat is built by: (1) consistently showing Tim as the face, not a brand voice; (2) using real dealer language — not marketing copy; (3) featuring actual dealer users and their actual results as the content, not endorsements; (4) building in public so the product's evolution becomes a content asset that competitors cannot replicate. The moat deepens the longer it runs — every piece of founder-authentic content from 2026 becomes a timestamp that proves Tim was here first, doing this for real, before any competitor thought to try it.
